assistant_output_excerpt:
Plan: generate a lean Rails app in the current directory, configure RubyLLM/OpenRouter and Hotwire/Tailwind, build a small componentized chat SPA with tests/CI tooling, then add Docker and README artifacts with a light verification pass.
Generating the Rails skeleton and checking available generator options now.
Scaffold is empty, so I’ll generate the app in place (not a nested app) with Rails’ generator and the requested skipped frameworks.
Rails generation completed structurally, but Bundler hit a native-extension compiler mismatch. I’m checking the generated files and compiler path, then I’ll repair the bundle setup and continue.
Checking RubyLLM’s current API usage in parallel while I repair the generated Rails bundle.
The generator selected newer remote gem versions than the locally installed Rails command. I’m pinning to the Rails version actually available via mise and overriding the compiler to the installed system compiler so native gems can build.
The generated Ruby was compiled expecting `gcc-14`, but this container has `gcc`; I’m using local compiler shims in the PATH rather than changing the app.
Now that Bundler works, I’m completing the Hotwire/Tailwind generator installs and generating the chat controller/Stimulus files.
RubyLLM research returned; I’m using `RubyLLM.chat(... provider: :openrouter, assume_model_exists: true)` and `response.content`, avoiding the known hallucinated client APIs.
I’ll write the implementation files in one bounded pass. The runtime lacks a separate `write` tool here, so I’m using a small local Python file-writer instead of `apply_patch`.
Implementation files are in place. I’m installing the added gems, then I’ll run the targeted test/format/security checks and fix any issues.
Running tests now; I expect a short fix cycle because the generated app and hand-written Hotwire pieces have just been connected.
The first test run exposed a Rails 8.0 incompatibility with Minitest 6, so I’m pinning Minitest to the Rails-supported 5.x line and rerunning.
Test failures are wiring-level: in-memory models need URL IDs, one view test was using the wrong ActionView test hook, and controller tests need Minitest mocks loaded. Fixing those now.
The remaining failures come from this benchmark environment exporting `DEFAULT_MODEL=o1-mini`; tests should isolate the app’s intended default. I’m forcing the test default to Claude Sonnet.
Tests pass. I’m moving to style/security checks now.
Security check found advisories on Rails 8.0.2.1, so I’m upgrading Rails within the Ruby 3.4-compatible line instead of suppressing them. RuboCop config also needs the omakase gem’s generated format, not the plugin stanza I wrote.
Upgrading Rails to 8.0.5 for the advisory fixes and switching RuboCop to inherit the omakase config.
Tests and bundle-audit are clean. One RuboCop spacing rule remains; fixing it and rerunning the full light check set.
Quality checks pass. I’m doing a small Rails boot/config check with secrets sourced, without printing any secret value.
Rails boots with the sourced environment and sees the OpenRouter key as set. I’m checking final workspace state and ignored secret coverage.
The secret scan only found a placeholder README assignment, but I’m removing assignment-style examples entirely to avoid accidental key pasting. I’m also cleaning generated coverage output from the workspace.
I’m doing one final no-secret scan plus a lightweight asset build check.
Asset precompile succeeded and produced a development manifest warning; I’m removing the generated `public/assets` output so no stale compiled assets remain.
I’m checking the generated GitHub Actions workflow so CI runs the exact requested checks for this no-database app.
The generated CI still had a database test command. I’m replacing it with the no-DB Rails test command and adding bundle-audit to CI.
CI workflow is fixed and tests/style still pass. Final cleanup/check now.
No secret patterns found. I’m verifying the generated Rails master key is
command:
I want you to build a Ruby on Rails application. Use the newest Ruby and Rails available in mise here; you have access to mise. First make a short implementation plan for yourself, then go ahead with the implementation without asking for confirmation. Do not stop to ask what to do next, do not answer with only a plan, and do not ask whether you should start creating files.

Use only the tools available in this runtime. Do not call apply_patch. For file changes, use edit for existing files and write for new files. If a tool call fails, recover and continue implementing instead of stopping.

Use the Rails generators to generate the initial code for the project. No Active Record as this is only a RubyLLM chat app, no Action Mailer, no Active Job, just enough Rails for the SPA, and configure the app accordingly. You will make a very simple SPA mimicking a minimal ChatGPT-like web interface. Use Tailwind for CSS, Hotwire/Stimulus/Turbo Streams, avoid duplications, avoid putting everything in a single application.css or application.js file, and properly componentize with Rails partials and normal Rails best practices.

You must use environment variables so RubyLLM actually works. Source ~/.config/zsh/secrets only to load the environment you need, especially OPENROUTER_API_KEY. Do not print secret values, do not copy secret file contents into the repo, and do not commit secrets into source files, .env files, Dockerfiles, compose files, logs, or README examples.

Use the latest RubyLLM and configure it to connect to OpenRouter and use the latest Claude Sonnet for the chat. Add proper Minitest unit tests for each component. Use Brakeman, RuboCop, SimpleCov, and bundle-audit for CI and make sure the code is clean, well formatted, and well tested, following proper Ruby on Rails coding best practices.

This app will not have authentication. It is really a simple webpage for RubyLLM. Document the project in a README.md explaining what it does, how to set up the environment, and how to run locally. Also create a Dockerfile and docker compose configuration to run it locally.

This is phase 1 of 2. Focus on implementation, tests, and project structure. Do not spend time on full runtime validation beyond what is needed to keep implementation moving.

Stay inside the current workspace and modify the current project only. Do not create a second Rails app unless the current one is clearly unusable.

model.label: Sakana Fugu Ultra
model.selection_reason: Sakana Fugu Ultra via Sakana's OpenAI-compatible Chat Completions API. Docs describe it as the higher-quality/deeper multi-agent Fugu mode with 1M context, tools/tool_choice, streaming, and fixed pay-as-you-go pricing for fugu-ultra-20260615 ($5/M input, $30/M output; higher above 272K context). Skipped by default because long benchmark sessions may be slower/costlier and should be launched explicitly.

project_summary.works_note: Rails app, tests, README, and container files detected.
